risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Families were stratified at each tribal community on the basis of child BMI percentile for age and gender... Within each stratum, half of the families were randomly assigned to the intervention condition and half to the control condition. Furthermore, within each stratum, a blocked randomization strategy was used to ensure that there was an equal number of families in the intervention and control groups." Comment: This describes a stratified, blocked randomization process with explicit random assignment, which is sufficient to judge the allocation sequence as truly random.

_evidence.method_quote: Randomization at the family level was done after obtaining consent from and completing baseline measurements on participating families. Families were stratified at each tribal community on the basis of child BMI percentile for age and gender, with two strata at each community: families with children BMIs < 85th percentile and families with children BMIs ≥ 85th percentile. Within each stratum, half of the families were randomly assigned to the intervention condition and half to the control condition. Furthermore, within each stratum, a blocked randomization strategy was used to ensure that there was an equal number of families in the intervention and control groups.
_evidence.method_summary: Families were stratified by community and child BMI percentile, then assigned within strata using blocked randomization to intervention or control.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The article describes stratified/block randomization after consent and baseline measures, but gives no explicit information about how upcoming assignments were concealed from the recruiters. Comment: Because allocation concealment requires an explicit mechanism preventing foreknowledge (e.g., central allocation or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes), and none is described here, the risk is unclear rather than low; there is also no positive evidence of an open or predictable allocation procedure to justify high risk.

_evidence.note: The article describes sequence generation and stratified/block randomization, but gives no details on how upcoming assignments were concealed from recruiters.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The article describes a family-based randomized trial with a home-visit mentor intervention versus mailed educational tool kits, but it does not state whether participants or personnel were blinded, nor does it describe any blinding method. Comment: Because blinding status is not specified, and although the study includes several subjective outcomes (e.g. health behavior efficacy, health-related quality of life, self-reported diet/screen time), there is insufficient positive evidence to judge performance bias as low or high.

Summary: The study reports a mix of objective outcomes and patient-reported/subjective outcomes, including "fruit/vegetable consumption," "soda/sweetened drink and candy consumption," "hours of TV viewing," "health-related quality of life," and "health behavior efficacy." These are assessed by the participant/primary caregiver (or proxy), so the patient is effectively the assessor. No blinding of outcome assessment is described, and participants in the intervention arm received home visits and group sessions while controls received mailed education only, so knowledge of allocation could influence self-reported outcome measurement. Comment: Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded subjective/patient-reported outcomes makes the whole domain High risk even though several biochemical and anthropometric outcomes are objective.

Summary: The article is a design/methods paper and does not report CONSORT-style participant flow, per-arm randomized vs analysed numbers, losses to follow-up, or reasons for missing data for the analysed outcomes. Comment: Because actual post-randomization attrition on the outcome time points cannot be computed from the supplied text, there is no positive evidence of substantial or differential attrition to justify High risk, and no evidence of complete follow-up to justify Low risk.

_evidence.notes: This article is a design/methods paper and reports planned analyses and outcome time points, but no CONSORT-style participant flow or analysed/lost numbers for the randomized groups.
